message.group-title: Intensive Care and Critical Care Medicine
message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Rationale</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The progress of COVID-19 from moderate to severe may be precipitous, while the heterogenous characteristics of the disease pose challenges to the management of these patients.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To characterize the clinical course and outcomes of critically ill patients with COVID-19 during two successive waves.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We leveraged the multi-center SuPAR in Adult Patients With COVID-19 (SPARCOL) study and collected data from consecutive patients requiring admission to the intensive care unit from April 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    to December 31
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    , 2020.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Measurements and Main Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Of 252 patients, 81 (32%) required intubation and mechanical ventilation. Of them, 17 (20.9%) were intubated during the first wave, while 64 (79%) during the second wave. The most prominent difference between the two waves was the overall survival (first wave 58.9%
                    <jats:italic>vs.</jats:italic>
                    second wave 15.6%, adjusted p-value=0.006). This difference is reflected in the prolonged hospitalization during the first wave. The mean ICU length of stay (19.1
                    <jats:italic>vs.</jats:italic>
                    11.7 days, p=0.022), hospital length of stay (28.5
                    <jats:italic>vs.</jats:italic>
                    17.1 days, p=0.012), and days on ventilator (16.7
                    <jats:italic>vs.</jats:italic>
                    11.5, p=0.13) were higher during the first wave. A significant difference between the two waves was the development of bradycardia. In the first wave, 2 (11.7%) patients developed sinus bradycardia only after admission to the intensive care unit, while in the second wave, 63 (98.4%) patients developed sinus bradycardia during hospitalization.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Survival of critically ill patients with COVID-19 was significantly lower during the second wave. The majority of these patients developed sinus bradycardia during hospitalization.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Clinical course and outcomes of critically ill COVID-19 patients in two successive pandemic waves
